retrieved_docs.title: Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Immunotherapy for NSCLC
retrieved_docs.content: Nivolumab is an IgG4 monoclonal antibody against PD-1. It is approved by the FDA for squamous and non-squamous NSCLC that has progressed after platinum-based chemotherapy. It can be used in patients with high or low PD-L1 expression status. Pembrolizumab is also an IgG4 monoclonal antibody against PD-1. It is approved for pre-treated metastatic NSCLC with greater than 50% expression of PD-L1 and does not harbor EGFR and ALK mutations. It is also combined with pemetrexed and carboplatin for metastatic non-squamous NSCLC with less than 50% expression of PD-L1. Atezolizumab is an IgG1 antibody against PD-L1. It is approved for use in metastatic, progressive NSCLC during or following treatment with platinum-based chemotherapy. It can be used in patients who express EGFR and ALK mutations and fail targeted therapy. Bevacizumab is not considered immune therapy. It is an anti-angiogenesis antibody that inhibits vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGF-A). It is primarily used in combination with platinum-based chemotherapy to treat non-squamous NSCLC. It is contraindicated in squamous cell NSCLC due to the risk of severe and often fatal hemoptysis. It is also used to treat breast, renal, colon, and brain cancers. [37] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If the patient is found to have positive margins, they would then require postoperative radiation therapy or reresection followed by adjuvant chemotherapy. If the patient is a clinical-stage I or II and deemed not a surgical candidate, then treatment would focus on stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) or definitive RT. Clinical stage III would indicate a multidisciplinary approach to treatment that would include consultation with medical oncology, radiation oncology, and thoracic surgery to decide on the optimal combined approach to the disease process. It is important for tissue biopsy to be analyzed for targetable mutation, which can aid in the treatment of patients with stage IV NSCLC. For example, if epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) positive, tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as osimertinib, erlotinib, gefitinib, or afatinib can be utilized in treatment. If the tumor contains anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) fusion oncogene, treatment with an ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitor is preferred, which can include alectinib, ceritinib, or brigatinib. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Bevacizumab -- Indications -- FDA-approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC): Bevacizumab is a first-line treatment combination with carboplatin and paclitaxel for recurrent, locally advanced, unresectable, or metastatic non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tyrosine Kinase Receptors -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Ramucirumab is the only monoclonal antibody targeting the VEGFR, inhibiting tumor angiogenesis. Specifically, it prevents VEGF binding to VEGFR-2. It is indicated only in advanced or metastatic colorectal, gastric, and non-small cell lung cancer that has progressed or relapsed after first-line therapy. Given this use, it has a more limited clinical significance. For example, in patients with advanced or metastatic gastric and gastroesophageal cancer who had failed fluoropyrimidine or platinum-based therapy, the median overall survival was 5.2 months in the ramucirumab group versus 3.8 months in the placebo group. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Cetuximab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content:
Cetuximab is an epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitor with the following FDA-approved indications: colorectal cancer, metastatic, KRAS wild-type (without mutation), and head and neck cancer (squamous cell). 
Non-FDA-approved uses include colorectal cancer, non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), EGFR-expressing, advanced, and squamous cell skin cancer. This activity reviews the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, toxicity, dosing, pharmacodynamics, and monitoring of cetuximab, pertinent for members of the interprofessional team for the treatment of patients with malignancies for which it is indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Leptomeningeal Carcinomatosis -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Bevacizumab (VGEF inhibitor) and dabrafenib (BRAF inhibitor) have been reported to demonstrate a response in LC from melanoma. IT trastuzumab in LC from HER-2 positive breast cancer also has shown some promise and a favorable adverse effect profile. There are several ongoing phase II trials on the subject. EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) has shown a response to erlotinib and gefitinib, but at higher doses, as they do not cross the BBB easily. There are ongoing trials of other tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) in LC from EGFR-mutant NSCLC that have shown promising results in terms of extended survival. Anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) inhibitors are another class of drugs shown to be effective in LC from NSCLC with ALK rearrangements in ongoing trials.

retrieved_docs.title: Trastuzumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non-small cell lung cancer: Trastuzumab deruxtecan is approved for HER2-mutant (unresectable or metastatic) NSCLC in adults who have previously received systemic therapy. [4] Per the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) 2024 guidelines regarding Stage IV Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer with driver alterations, clinicians may offer treatment with trastuzumab deruxtecan. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Non–Small Cell Lung Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A variety of other mutations, including ROS1, BRAF, RET, TRK, MET, KRAS, can also be found, and for these, their specific inhibitors should be integrated into the treatment plan. If a driver mutation is absent or unknown, the level of programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1) expression should be quantified, and if greater than 50 percent, then pembrolizumab or atezolizumab may be utilized in the treatment plan. These can also be used as single-agent treatment without chemotherapy. [17] Lastly, patients should be actively assessed for eligibility for clinical trials during their disease course.

retrieved_docs.title: Atezolizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of atezolizumab and bevacizumab plus chemotherapy (eg, paclitaxel and carboplatin) is the first-line treatment for adults with metastatic non-squamous NSCLC regardless of PD-L1 expression and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) or anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) genomic tumor alterations.

retrieved_docs.content: Malignant pleural mesothelioma [2] [5] [6] [7] First-line treatment for stage I to stage IIIA, regardless of histologic type (as monotherapy) First-line treatment for unresectable tumors, regardless of histologic type or patients who are not candidates for surgery (in combination with cisplatin) As an adjuvant medication Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), nonsquamous [8] [9] [10] [11] Locally advanced or metastatic, nonsquamous NSCLC (in combination with cisplatin) Metastatic, nonsquamous NSCLC in patients with no epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) or anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) genomic tumor aberrations (in combination with platinum chemotherapy and pembrolizumab) Maintenance treatment in patients whose disease has not progressed after 4 cycles of platinum-based chemotherapy Patients with a history of prior chemotherapy (as monotherapy)

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Treatment Fields in Oncology -- Indications -- Non-small Cell Lung Carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: The efficacy of TTF has been shown in animal models of advanced non-small cell lung carcinoma. Human studies have demonstrated the safety of the treatment, but its efficacy is still under evaluation. Ongoing LUNAR phase III trial aims to evaluate the efficacy of TTF with docetaxel in non-small cell lung carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.content: Non–small cell lung cancer Metastatic, with progression during or after platinum-based chemotherapy (and after progression under targeted therapies for EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations in case these aberrations are present). [2] As first-line therapy combined with ipilimumab in metastatic or recurrent non–small cell lung cancer with a PD-L1 expression equal or higher to 1% with no EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations. [3] As first-line therapy in combination with ipilimumab, given with 2 cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy. Renal cell carcinoma After prior anti­angiogenic therapy. Previously untreated, of intermediate or poor risk, in combination with ipilimumab. [5] Pleural Mesothelioma In combination with ipilimumab for unresectable pleural mesothelioma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies have now been approved for treating advanced colorectal and head and neck tumors, and EGFR TKIs have been approved for treating advanced non–small cell lung cancer, pancreatic carcinoma, head and neck tumors, and glioblastoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride is an anthracycline chemotherapy agent that intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, while its liposomal formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome) offers altered pharmacokinetics and reduced cardiotoxicity for various solid tumors and hematologic malignancies. Pembrolizumab is a programmed death‑1 (PD‑1) immune checkpoint inhibitor that enhances antitumor immunity in a wide range of cancers, including melanoma, non‑small cell lung cancer, and urothelial carcinoma. Sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor (GM‑CSF) used to accelerate recovery of white blood cells after chemotherapy or bone‑marrow transplantation. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: Cabozantinib and midostaurin are both classified as protein kinase inhibitors within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system under the category “Other protein kinase inhibitors.” This classification indicates that each drug belongs to the same therapeutic group of agents that target protein kinases, reflecting shared mechanisms of action and relevance in the treatment of diseases such as cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 723
retrieved_docs.content: and chlormethin hydrochlorid further contribute to the chemotherapeutic arsenal. Targeted and immunomodulatory therapies are highlighted by brentuximab vedotin (a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate), pembrolizumab (a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor) and sargramostim (a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor), each directing immune mechanisms against Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a multimodal treatment paradigm that integrates alkylators, platinum compounds, anthracyclines, antimitotics, antibiotics, monoclonal antibodies and immune‑stimulating agents—used alone or in combination—to manage Hodgkin disease, underscoring the importance of diverse chemotherapy regimens and emerging immunotherapies in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1254
retrieved_docs.content: Cisplatin, dakarbazin, lomustine, vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlormethine hydrochloride, doxorubicin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, sargramostim and vinblastine sulfate are all agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين). These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including platinum‑based compounds (cisplatin), alkylating agents (dakarbazin, lomustine, chlormethine hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide), anthracycline antibiotics (doxorubicin hydrochloride), vinca alkaloids (vincristine sulfate, vinblastine sulfate), antitumor antibiotics (bleomycin sulfate), a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), and a cytokine growth factor (sargramostim). Their combined or individual use in chemotherapy protocols reflects current medical practice for managing Hodgkin disease, aiming to eradicate malignant Reed‑Sternberg cells, achieve remission, and improve long‑term patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, mechlorethamine, vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, chlormethin hydrochloride, liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide, and pembrolizumab are all agents that may be used to treat مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, mechlorethamine, procarbazine hydrochloride, ifosfamide), a microtubule inhibitor (vincristine sulfate), a DNA‑intercalating anthracycline formulation (liposomal doxorubicin hydrochloride), a bleomycin family antitumor antibiotic (bleomycin sulfate), a targeted antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), a miscellaneous cytotoxic compound (chlormethin hydrochloride), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab). Their combined inclusion reflects the multimodal approach often employed in managing Hodgkin disease, where chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy may be selected based on disease stage, patient characteristics, and prior treatment response.
